title: Bad debt fears help carve $30b off CBA
full_text:
Disappointing earnings, rising bad debts and fears that Labor's tax concession changes will slash demand for loans has led to an investor exodus from Commonwealth Bank, with shares in the country's largest lender suffering their biggest one-day fall since the pandemic.
The slump began with a third-quarter cash profit of $2.7 billion, which missed market expectations, and accelerated as worries grew about how changes unveiled by Treasurer Jim Chalmers would hit bank profits.
Jarden described the changes to negative gearing and capital gains taxes as "not ... that friendly to major banks", and said CBA would be most exposed, given its 26 per cent share of the property investor mortgage market.
The market had already been skittish about rising bad debts, with CBA's rivals putting aside hundreds of millions of dollars in extra provisions for loans that could sour as the war in the Middle East increased costs, and the Reserve Bank of Australia continued to push up interest rates.
CBA followed its peers in lifting buffers by $200 million. "The Australian economy continues to demonstrate resilience, but supply chain disruptions, higher prices and interest rates are expected to weigh on household spending and business activity," said CBA chief executive Matt Comyn.
Personal loan arrears hit their highest level since 2019, while home loan and credit card arrears also increased. "Troublesome and non-performing" business loans of $6.5 billion were $400 million higher than the previous quarter, reflecting "single name exposures across a range of sectors".
Cash profit rose 4 per cent in the three months to March 31 compared with the same time last year, missing market expectations by about 2 per cent.
Home loan balances grew by $41 billion over the past 12 months, in line with the broader industry, while household deposits increased by $38 billion. Lending to businesses grew by $22 billion over the same period.
CBA said lending margins were "broadly stable", as competition in home and business lending persisted, while operating expenses were up 1 per cent as the bank lifted spending on cloud computing and AI.
Shares fell 10.4 per cent to close $17.90 lower at $153.67. That slump wiped more than $30 billion from the bank's market capitalisation.
"The combination of last night's budget with a quarterly on the softer side, and not as clean as what we have come to expect, can really weigh on the stock, given how expensive CBA is," said Michael Haynes, an analyst at Atlas Funds Management, which has been a long-term investor in the bank.
Labor, in its budget this week, ended preferential tax treatment for property investors as part of attempts at improving housing affordability. The government will scrap the CGT exemption for assets held before 1985, and introduce a minimum rate of 30 per cent to discourage people holding on to property in retirement. While this change will reduce housing supply by 35,000 homes over the next decade, the government says this will be more than offset by measures creating 65,000 extra dwellings.
Negative gearing will be abolished for existing houses, effective immediately in a change that analysts pointed to as particularly bad for banks.
"CBA appears quite vulnerable to the negative gearing changes for investor home loans, a space it dominates, where loans are typically interest only, wider spread and better asset quality underpinning a superior [return]," Jarden's Matthew Wilson said.
"[CBA] confronts the same sector challenges - low growth, declining returns, increasing credit risk - and, moreover, is the most vulnerable in our view to disruption from IT innovation."
Nicola Powell, the head of research at real estate listings portal Domain, said the end of the 50 per cent CGT discount on investments and the imposition of restrictions on negative gearing would dampen appetite.
"Ultimately, it will make property less attractive as an asset to invest in," she said. "For those who want to stay in property, it will divert investors towards higher-yielding, more affordable property markets."
Others also fell, with National Australia Bank down 1.5 per cent to $36.86, ANZ off 1.6 per cent to $34.57 and Westpac falling 2.8 per cent to $35.57.
The major banks reported a combined profit after tax of $15.2 billion for the first half of the financial year, down 2.1 per cent on the prior first half.
David Heathcote, who leads KPMG's banking practice, said CBA's quarterly figures should not have come as a surprise after other banks reported.
"Given the outlook for increased interest rates impacting discretionary spending, it seems sensible to be lifting provisions, and the level of credit provisioning looks appropriate," Heathcote said, adding that investor worries were focused on bad debts in business lending along with the uncertainties created by Labor's tax concession changes.
summary: Commonwealth Bank shares dropped 10.4% after disappointing earnings and fears that new tax changes will hurt loan demand. The bank's cash profit of $2.7 billion missed expectations, leading to concerns about rising bad debts and increased provisions for loans.
author: James Eyers
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: CBA's shares fell to $153.67, losing over $30 billion in market value.
The bank's cash profit for Q3 was $2.7 billion, 4% higher year-on-year but below market expectations.
Rising bad debts and changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax are causing investor concerns.
CBA increased its loan provisions by $200 million amid economic pressures.
Other major banks also saw share price declines following CBA's news.
ticker_line: CBA shares plunge amid bad debt fears
